data.title: test-writer: orchestrator proposed change-detector test for LLM behavioral instruction placement
data.description:
## Expected Behavior
When a code change is to a markdown instruction file (skill/agent prompt), and the change is LLM behavioral compliance (instruction placement, wording), the orchestrator must recognize this as infeasible to test via structural/grep tests and must not propose or create any test.

## Actual Behavior
After adding a per-question investigation directive inside Phase 1 Step 2 of brainstorm/SKILL.md, the orchestrator proposed the following test: extract the Step 2 block using awk, then grep for 'DO NOT' language within that block, and assert it is present. This is a change-detector test — it would fail any time the exact wording or position changes, even if the behavioral intent is preserved with different phrasing.

## What was proposed
The orchestrator stated: 'I'll add a test that extracts the Step 2 block and checks for the DO NOT directive as the structural signal that the per-question constraint is present.' The proposed test would use awk to extract the Step 2 section and grep for specific body text ('DO NOT ask questions whose answers are discoverable').

## Why it is wrong
1. Behavioral testing standard Rule 5: for instruction files, test structural boundaries (section headings), not content.
2. Any grep for specific body text in a markdown instruction file is a change-detector — it locks in exact wording.
3. LLM behavioral compliance (whether agents follow an instruction) is infeasible to test in this test system.
4. The user explicitly stopped the action and corrected: 'We have a rule against change detector tests. This change is exempted from testing because it is infeasible to test LLM behavior in our test system.'

## Root Cause
The orchestrator's reflex to 'always add a test' overrides judgment about whether a test is feasible or appropriate for a given change type. Behavioral instruction placement changes in markdown files do not have a valid mechanical test counterpart.
